choices.prompt: According to the module, how many triggers can a single Azure Function have?
choices: Exactly one trigger, which defines the event that starts it and the data delivered to it
At most two triggers, so it can respond to both a schedule and a queue
Any number of triggers, as long as they share the same connection setting
One trigger plus one optional fallback trigger for cold-start scenarios
correct_answers: Exactly one trigger, which defines the event that starts it and the data delivered to it

choices.prompt: Northwind Parcel needs a job that expires stale carts every night at 02:00 UTC. Which trigger does the module use for this, and why is it the right fit?
choices: A timer trigger, because it runs code on a CRON-style schedule with no inbound request needed
An HTTP trigger, because the nightly sweep must be invoked by a scheduled web request
A queue trigger, because a scheduler service drops a message that the function consumes
A blob trigger, because the cart records appear as files in storage each night
correct_answers: A timer trigger, because it runs code on a CRON-style schedule with no inbound request needed

choices.prompt: In the walkthrough's MakeLabelThumbnail function, what does the single line `return thumbnail_bytes` actually accomplish?
choices: The blob output binding routes the returned bytes to a generated .png in the thumbnails container, so the return is the whole persistence step
It hands the bytes back to the queue trigger, which re-enqueues them for a later write
It opens a blob client, uploads the bytes, and closes the connection before exiting
It signals the runtime to retry the function because no output binding is declared
correct_answers: The blob output binding routes the returned bytes to a generated .png in the thumbnails container, so the return is the whole persistence step

choices.prompt: Your HTTP-triggered function on a Consumption plan is occasionally slow on the first call after a quiet night. Per the module, what is happening and what is one way to address it?
choices: A cold start: the plan scaled to zero and must spin up an instance; use a Premium/dedicated (warm) plan or a warming strategy
A timeout: the plan killed the idle instance mid-flight; raise the function's per-invocation timeout limit
Throttling: the Consumption plan rate-limits the first daily request; add a second trigger to share the load
A binding failure: the input binding re-fetches data on the first call; cache the connection string in code
correct_answers: A cold start: the plan scaled to zero and must spin up an instance; use a Premium/dedicated (warm) plan or a warming strategy

choices.prompt: Which of the following statements about triggers and bindings are correct according to the module? (Select all that apply.)
choices: An input binding hands the function data fetched from an external source before it runs
A function has one trigger but can have several input and output bindings
The `connection` property must name an app setting rather than contain a literal connection string
Every input and output binding counts as an additional trigger for the function
correct_answers: An input binding hands the function data fetched from an external source before it runs
A function has one trigger but can have several input and output bindings
The `connection` property must name an app setting rather than contain a literal connection string

choices.prompt: According to the module, what triggers a queue or blob trigger to run, and what kind of coupling does this enable between parts of a system?
choices: A message landing on a queue or a file appearing in storage runs it, letting one part of the system react to another asynchronously
An inbound HTTP request runs it, letting the caller wait synchronously for the result
A timer firing on a CRON schedule runs it, letting jobs react to one another on a fixed cadence
An output binding completing runs it, letting one function chain directly into the next
correct_answers: A message landing on a queue or a file appearing in storage runs it, letting one part of the system react to another asynchronously

choices.prompt: The module warns against putting long-running work in a single function invocation. What is the recommended way to handle a job that would otherwise exceed the plan's timeout?
choices: Break the long work into queue-driven steps so each invocation is short and restartable
Move the job to an HTTP trigger so the request can stay open until the work finishes
Run the job on the Consumption plan so scale-to-zero pauses and resumes it as needed
Add a second trigger to the function so the work is split across both invocations
correct_answers: Break the long work into queue-driven steps so each invocation is short and restartable

choices.prompt: Per the module, what are the defining cost-and-scaling characteristics of the Consumption plan, and what is the trade-off it carries?
choices: It scales to zero and bills per execution, ideal for spiky infrequent work, but an idle function may incur a cold start
It keeps instances warm at a higher baseline cost, eliminating cold starts for all functions
It bills a flat monthly rate regardless of executions, making cost predictable but never scaling down
It scales to zero only for timer triggers, while HTTP triggers always keep one instance warm
correct_answers: It scales to zero and bills per execution, ideal for spiky infrequent work, but an idle function may incur a cold start

choices.prompt: A teammate sets `connection="DefaultEndpointsProtocol=https;AccountName=..."` directly on a queue trigger. According to the module's pitfalls, what is wrong and what should be done instead? (Select all that apply.)
choices: The `connection` property must name an app setting, not contain the literal value
Putting a literal connection string there leaks the secret
It breaks per-environment configuration
A literal connection string is acceptable as long as it is the production storage account
correct_answers: The `connection` property must name an app setting, not contain the literal value
Putting a literal connection string there leaks the secret

choices.prompt: Based on the walkthrough and deployment steps, which of the following are accurate? (Select all that apply.)
choices: In the v2 programming model, triggers and bindings are declared with decorators
The function app is deployed with `func azure functionapp publish`
The timer-triggered ExpireStaleCarts function never opens a connection to anything; it just runs on schedule
STORAGE_CONNECTION holds the literal storage connection string embedded in the function source code
correct_answers: In the v2 programming model, triggers and bindings are declared with decorators
The function app is deployed with `func azure functionapp publish`
The timer-triggered ExpireStaleCarts function never opens a connection to anything; it just runs on schedule

llm.prompt: Explain why running Northwind Parcel's background jobs (cart expiry, payment webhook, label thumbnailing) inside a continuously-billed App Service web app is wasteful, and how the Azure Functions serverless model changes the picture.
llm.reference_answer: These jobs do nothing most of the time, yet a web app keeps a process alive and bills for a server that is always on, so you pay for idle capacity. Azure Functions follows the serverless model: code wakes up only when an event occurs, runs, and then gets out of the way, and you are billed for what it does rather than for being available. The key shift is that with App Service you keep a process alive to wait for requests, whereas with Functions the platform watches the event source and invokes your code on your behalf. On the Consumption plan this is reinforced by scaling to zero and billing per execution, which fits spiky, infrequent background work like these jobs.

llm.prompt: A teammate needs the same logic to run both at midnight and whenever a message arrives on a queue, and writes one function trying to attach both triggers. Walk through why this fails and the clean design the module recommends.
llm.reference_answer: It fails because a function is defined by exactly one trigger; you cannot stack a timer trigger and a queue trigger on a single function. The clean design is to factor the shared work into a helper function, then write two thin trigger functions that both call it: one timer-triggered to run at midnight on a CRON schedule, and one queue-triggered to run when a message lands on the queue. This keeps the one-trigger-per-function rule intact while reusing the business logic, since reuse comes from factoring logic rather than from stacking triggers. Each trigger still defines the event that starts the function and the data delivered to it.

llm.prompt: Compare doing connection plumbing by hand versus using an output binding for the label-thumbnail function, and explain the role STORAGE_CONNECTION plays in keeping the secret safe.
llm.reference_answer: Doing it by hand means manually opening a blob client, uploading the result, and closing the connection; it works but you lose declarative clarity and the runtime's connection management. With a blob output binding declared in a decorator, the function body reduces to computing the result and returning it, and the runtime writes that return value to a generated .png in the thumbnails container, so the binding writes the boilerplate while you write only business logic. The module recommends preferring the output binding unless you need fine-grained control. The `connection="STORAGE_CONNECTION"` property must name an app setting that holds the storage connection string, not contain the literal value; this keeps the secret out of code and preserves per-environment configuration, whereas hard-coding a literal connection string would leak the secret and break that configuration.
